titleStmt.title: SII 2.73: original edition by Eugen Julius Theodor Hultzsch
No. 73. KASAKUDI PLATES OF NANDIVARMAN PALLAVAMALLA.

summary.p: On the 30th April 1891, Professor Julien Vinson, of Paris, was good enough to send me a reprintThe pages are numbered 433 to 469. of his paper Spأ©cimen de Palأ©ographie Tamoule, which contains an analysis of, and extracts from, the subjoined copper-plate inscription. The original plates had been discovered in 1879 at Kaإ›ؤپkإ«ل¸چi, 4 kilometres from Kؤپraikkؤپl (Karikal),On copper coins which bear the name of this place in Tamil characters, see Ind. Ant., Vol. XXI, p. 327. See also p. 295 above, note 2. by M. Jules de la Fon, of Pondicherry. Professor Vinson's paper, which is based on a tracing prepared by M. de la Fon, convinced me of the importance of the inscription and induced me to apply through Government to His Excellency the Governor of the French Settlements in India for a loan of the original plates. This request was most graciously and promptly complied with. After I had transcribed the plates and prepared impressions of them, they were returned to their present owner.

The Kaإ›ؤپkإ«ل¸چi copper-plates, eleven in number, are strung on a ring. On this is soldered the royal seal, with the figure of a bull which faces the left and is surmounted by a liل¹…ga. The bull was the crest of the Pallavas,See verses 9 and 24 of this inscription, and Vol. I, p. 23, note 2. while their banner bore the figure of إڑiva's club (khaل¹­vؤپل¹…ga).See verse 24, and Vol. I, p. 146. The Grantha and Tamil characters of the inscription resemble those of the Kإ«ram plates (Vol. I, No. 151). The major portion of the inscription is in the Sanskrit language (lines 1 to 104). The particulars of the grant are repeated, with considerable additions, in the Tamil language (ll. 104 to 133). The concluding portion of the inscription is again in Sanskrit (ll. 133 to 138), with a short parenthetical note in Tamil (l. 137).

The immediate object of the inscription is to record the grant of a village, made in the 22nd year of the reign (ll. 80 and 105) of the Pallava king Nandivarman (verses 27 and 30, and l. 79). As in other Pallava copper-plate inscriptions, the grant proper is preceded by a panegyrical account of the king's ancestors, which adds a large number of new details to our knowledge of the Pallava history. After nine benedictory verses, the author names the following mythical ancestors of the Pallava dynasty:â€”

This last king can scarcely be considered a historical person, but appears to be a modification of the ancient Maurya king Aإ›إچka. Then follows a passage in prose, which informs us that, after this Aإ›إچkavarman, there ruled a number of other Pallava kings, viz., [S]kandavarman, Kal[i]ndavarman, Kؤپل¹‡agإچpa, Vishل¹‡ugإچpa, Vؤ«rakإ«[r]cha, Vؤ«rasiل¹پha, Siل¹پhavarman, Vishل¹‡usiل¹پha and others (l. 48 f.). Some of these names actually occur in the inscriptions of that ancient branch of the Pallavas, whose grants are dated from Palakkada, Daإ›anapura and Kؤپأ±chؤ«pura, viz., Skandavarman, Siل¹پhavarman, Vishل¹‡ugإچpavarman,Dr. Fleet's Kanarese Dynasties, p. 16. Vishل¹‡ugإچpa of Kؤپأ±chؤ« was a contemporary of Samudragupta (Gupta Inscriptions, p. 13). A Prؤپkل¹›it grant of إڑiva-Skandavarman, a Pallava king of Kؤپأ±chؤ«, has been published by Dr. Bu7hler (Ep. Ind., Vol. I, p. 2 ff.). Another Prؤپkل¹›it grant (Ind. Ant., Vol. IX, p. 100 ff.) belongs to the reign of Vijaya-Skandavarman. An archaic Chإچل¸·a inscription at Tirukkaل¹›ukkuل¹‰ل¹ںam mentions Skandaإ›ishya, who was probably a Pallava king (Ep. Ind., Vol. III, p. 277). and Vؤ«rakإچrchavarman.Ep. Ind., Vol. I, p. 397 f. The Amarؤپvatؤ« pillar-inscription (Vol. I, No. 32) mentions two kings named Siل¹پhavarman. But the order in which these names are enumerated, is completely different in each of the three available sources for the history of the early Pallavas, viz., the Amarؤپvatؤ« pillar, the early copper-inscriptions, and the prose introduction of the Kaإ›ؤپkإ«ل¸چi plates. For this reason, and on account of the summary manner in which the early kings are referred to by the author of the Kaإ›ؤپkإ«ل¸چi inscription, it is a mistake to derive a regular pedigree from the latter, as was done by Professor Vinson (l.c., p. 453); and it must be rather concluded that, at the time of Nandivarman, nothing was known of the predecessors of Siل¹پhavishل¹‡u, but the names of some of them, and that the order of their succession, and their relation to each other and to the subsequent line of Siل¹پhavishل¹‡u, were then entirely forgotten.

With verse 20 we enter on historical ground. The list of kings from Siل¹پhavishل¹‡u to the immediate predecessor of Nandivarman agrees with the Udayؤ“ndiram plates of Nandivarman Pallavamalla (No. 74). Siل¹پhavishل¹‡u appears to have borne the surname Avanisiل¹پha, and is stated to have defeated the Malaya, Kaل¸·abhra, Mؤپlava, Chإچل¸·a, Pؤپل¹‡ل¸چya, Siل¹پhaل¸·a and Kؤ“raل¸·a kings.

His successor Mahؤ“ndravarman I. â€œannihilated his chief enemies at Puل¸·ل¸·alإ«raâ€‌ (v. 21). The â€کchief enemiesâ€™ were probably the Chalukyas, who, in their turn, considered the Pallavas their â€کnatural enemies.â€™See Vol. I, p. 146, text line 38. As Puل¸·ل¸·alإ«r is the name of a village in the Conjeeveram tؤپlluqa,No. 182 on the Conjeeveram Taluk Map. it appears that the Chalukya army had made an inroad into the Pallava dominions, before it was repulsed by Mahؤ“ndravarman I.

His son Narasiل¹پhavarman I. is reported to have conquered Laل¹…kؤپ, i.e., Ceylon, and to have captured Vؤپtؤپpi,From a Tirukkaل¹›ukkuل¹‰ل¹ںam inscription we learn that he assumed after this conquest the surname Vؤپtؤپpi-koل¹‡ل¸چa, â€کwho took Vؤپtؤپpi;â€™ see Ep. Ind., Vol. III, p. 277. the capital of the Western Chalukyas. The Kإ«ram and Udayؤ“ndiram plates supply the name of the conquered Chalukya king, Pulakؤ“إ›in or Vallabharؤپja, i.e., Pulikؤ“إ›in II.See Vol. I, p. 145. The conquest of Ceylon to which the Kaإ›ؤپkإ«ل¸چi plates refer, is confirmed from an unexpected source. From the 47th chapter of the Mahؤپvaل¹پsaWijesinha's Translation of the Mahؤپvaل¹پsa, p. 41 ff. This reference was first noticed by Mr. Venkayya; see Ep. Ind., Vol. III, p. 277. we learn that the Singhalese prince Mؤپل¹‡avamma lived at the court of king Narasؤ«ha of India and helped him to crush his enemy, king Vallabha. The grateful Narasؤ«ha supplied Mؤپل¹‡avamma twice with an army to invade Ceylon. The second attack was successful. Mؤپل¹‡avamma occupied Ceylon, over which he is supposed to have ruled from A.D. 691 to 726. As both the Pallava inscriptions and the Mahؤپvaل¹پsa mention the war with Vallabha and the conquest of Ceylon, the identity of Narasؤ«ha and Narasiل¹پhavarman I. can hardly be doubted. As, however, the latest date of Pulikؤ“إ›in II. is A.D. 642,Ep. Ind., Vol. III, p. 2, Table. the accession of Mؤپل¹‡avamma must have taken place about half a century before A.D. 691.In my Annual Report for 1891-92, p. 5, footnote, I have noted a similar error of about half a century in the Singhalese chronology for the period between Rؤپjؤ“ndra-Chإچل¸·a I. and Kulإچttuل¹…ga-Chإچل¸·a I.

No details are given about the reign of Narasiل¹پhavarman's son Mahؤ“ndravarman II. The latter was succeeded by his son Paramؤ“إ›varapإچtavarman I. who, as we know from the Kإ«ram and Udayؤ“ndiram plates, defeated the Western Chalukya king Vikramؤپditya I. at Peruvaل¸·anallإ«r. The Kaإ›ؤپkإ«ل¸چi plates do not contain any historical information about him, nor about his son Narasiل¹پhavarman II. and his grandson Paramؤ“إ›varapإچtavarman II.

According to the Udayؤ“ndiram plates, the next king, Nandivarman, was the son of Paramؤ“إ›varavarman II. The Kaإ›ؤپkإ«ل¸چi plates contain an entirely different account of Nandivarman's parentage. In line 72, he professes to be â€œengaged in ruling the kingdom of Paramؤ“إ›varapإچtarؤپja;â€‌ and in verse 27, he is said to be ruling, at the time of the inscription, the kingdom of Paramؤ“إ›varapإچtavarman II., i.e., to have succeeded or supplanted the latter on the throne, and to have been â€œchosen by the subjects.â€‌ This plebiscite may have taken place after the death of the legitimate king; or, more probably, Nandivarman may have been an usurper who ousted and destroyed him and his family. At any rate, he was a remote kinsman of his predecessor. For, he was the son of Hiraل¹‡ya (verses 9 and 30) by Rإچhiل¹‡ؤ« and belonged to the branch (varga) of Bhؤ«ma (verse 30). According to verse 28, this branch of Bhؤ«ma took its origin from Bhؤ«mavarman, who was the younger brother of Siل¹پhavishل¹‡u. The names of three princes who intervened between Bhؤ«mavarman and Hiraل¹‡ya, are recorded in the same verse. The name Hiraل¹‡yavarma-Mahؤپrؤپja occurs several times in a much obliterated inscription of the Vaikuل¹‡ل¹­ha-Perumؤپل¸· temple at Kؤپnchؤ«puram. At the beginning of this inscription, Paramؤ“إ›varappإچttaraiyar of the Pallava-vaل¹پإ›a is mentioned as deceased (svargastha). It is therefore not improbable that the inscription recorded the accession of Hiraل¹‡yavarman or of his son Nandivarman after the death of Paramؤ“إ›varapإچtavarman II. The latter may have been the founder of the Vaikuل¹‡ل¹­ha-Perumؤپل¸· temple, which is called Paramؤ“إ›vara-Vishل¹‡ugل¹›iha, i.e., â€کthe Vishل¹‡u temple of Paramؤ“إ›vara,â€™ in another inscription of the Vaikuل¹‡ل¹­ha-Perumؤپل¸· temple.This fragment contains the date â€¢ â€¢ â€¢ rmmamahؤپrؤپjarkku yؤپل¹‡ل¹­u patineل¸»ؤپvatu, and refers immediately after to a golden vessel given to the temple by Danti[va]rma-Mahؤپrؤپja. One of the Vaishل¹‡ava hymns of the Nؤپlؤپyiraprabandham glorifies the temple of Paramؤ“إ›vara-Viل¹‡ل¹‡agaram at Kachchi (i.e., Kؤپأ±chؤ«), by which the Vaikuل¹‡ل¹­ha-Perumؤپل¸· temple must be meant. With the addition of the new branch, the list of the later Pallavas stands as follows:â€”
summary.table: [C1]1. Siل¹پhavishل¹‡u. [C1]2. Mahؤ“ndravarman I. [C2]Bhؤ«mavarman. [C1]3. Narasiل¹پhavarman I. [C2]Buddhavarman. [C1]4. Mahؤ“ndravarman II. [C2]ؤ€dityavarman. [C1]5. Paramؤ“إ›varapإچtavarman or Paramؤ“إ›varavarman I. [C2]Gإچvindavarman. [C1]6. Narasiل¹پhavarman II. [C2]Hiraل¹‡ya. [C1]7. Paramؤ“إ›varapإچtavarman or Paramؤ“إ›varavarman II. [C2]8. Nandivarman.
summary.p: Other forms of the name Nandivarman are Nandipإچtarؤپja (l. 90) and simply Nandin (l. 88). The form Nandipإچtavarman occurs in the Vakkalؤ“ri plates,Vol. I, p. 145 f. which refer to the defeat of the Pallava king by the Western Chalukya king Vikramؤپditya II., and the form Nandippإچttaraiyar in an inscription of his 18th year in the Ulagaل¸·anda-Perumؤپل¸· temple at Kؤپأ±chؤ«puram.See p. 341, note 3. He bore the sovereign titles Mahؤپrؤپja and Rؤپjؤپdhirؤپja-paramؤ“إ›vara and the birudas Kshatriyamalla, Pallavamalla (l. 78), and إڑrؤ«dhara (verse 29). According to verse 30, he was a devotee of Vishل¹‡u. At the request of his prime-minister (l. 89), Brahmaإ›rؤ«rؤپja (l. 91) or Brahmayuvarؤپja (ll. 103 and 106), the king gave the village of Koل¸چukoل¸·ل¸·i (ll. 99, 105 f.) to the Brؤپhmaل¹‡a Jyؤ“shل¹­hapؤپda-Sإچmayؤپjin (l. 93) or (in Tamil) إڑؤ“ل¹­ل¹­iل¹ںeل¹…ga-Sإچmayؤپjin (l. 108 f.), who belonged to the Bharadvؤپja (l. 94) or Bhؤپradvؤپja (l. 108) gإچtra, followed the Chhandإچgasإ«tra (ll. 94 and 108), and resided at Pإ«niya (l. 95) or Pإ«ni (l. 108), a village in the Toل¹‡ل¸چؤپka-rؤپshل¹­ra (l. 95). The village of Koل¸چukoل¸·ل¸·i, on becoming a brahmadؤ“ya, received the new name ؤ’kadhؤ«ramaل¹…galam (l. 100). It belonged to إھل¹ںل¹ںukkؤپل¹­ل¹­u-kإچل¹­ل¹­am (l. 105) or (in Sanskrit) Undivanakإچshل¹­haka (l. 98), a subdivision of Toل¹‡ل¸چؤپka-rؤپshل¹­ra, and was bounded in the east and south by Pؤپlaiyإ«r, in the west by Maل¹‡aل¹ںpؤپkkam and Koل¸·ل¸·ipؤپkkam, and in the north by Veل¸·imؤپnallإ«r (ll. 98 f. and 111 ff.). Connected with the gift of the village was the right to dig channels from the إڑؤ“yؤپل¹ںu or (in Sanskrit) Dإ«rasarit, the Veل¸¥kؤپ or Vؤ“gavatؤ«, and the tank of Tؤ«raiyaل¹‰ or Tؤ«ralaya (ll. 101 f. and 115 ff.).

Of these geographical names, the following can be identified. Toل¹‡ل¸چؤپka-rؤپshل¹­ra is,â€” like Toل¹‡ل¸چؤ«ra-maل¹‡ل¸چala, Tuل¹‡ل¸چؤ«ra-maل¹‡ل¸چala and Tuل¹‡ل¸چؤپka-vishaya,See p. 312, note 6. â€”a Sanskritised form of the Tamil term Toل¹‡ل¸چai-maل¹‡ل¸چalam. One of the 24 ancient divisions (kإچل¹­ل¹­am) of the latter was إھل¹ںل¹ںukkؤپل¹­ل¹­u-kإچل¹­ل¹­am, which owed its name to إھل¹ںل¹ںukkؤپل¸چu, a village in the present Conjeeveram tؤپlluqa.No. 112 on the Conjeeveram Taluk Map. This kإچل¹­ل¹­am was divided into four subdivisions (nؤپل¸چu), one of which was Pؤپlaiyإ«r-nؤپل¸چu.See Mr. Crole's Chingleput District Manual, p. 438. The head-village of this subdivision, Pؤپlaiyإ«r, appears to be identical with the village of Pؤپlaiyإ«r, which formed the south-eastern boundary of the granted village, and perhaps with the modern Pؤپlإ«r at the north-western extremity of the Chingleput tؤپlluqa.No. 71 on the Chingleput Taluk Map. The western boundary of the granted village, Maل¹‡aل¹ںpؤپkkam, would then be represented by the modern Mؤ“lamaل¹‡appؤپkkam.No. 72 on the same map. For the granted village, Koل¸چukoل¸·ل¸·i, itself and for the two remaining villages which formed its boundaries, no equivalents are found on the maps at my disposal. The village at which the donee resided, Pإ«ni, may be the modern Pإ«ل¹‡ل¸چi, which belongs to the Conjeeveram tؤپlluqa,No. 341 on the Conjeeveram Taluk Map. but is in close proximity of Pؤپlإ«r and Mؤ“lamaل¹‡appؤپkkam in the Chingleput tؤپlluqa. The proposed identification of these three villages is made more probable by the reference, made in the Kaإ›ؤپkإ«ل¸چi plates, to two rivers near which the granted village of Koل¸چukoل¸·ل¸·i was situated. Of these, the Vؤ“gavatؤ« or Veل¸¥kؤپ passes Conjeeveram and falls into the Pؤپlؤپل¹ںu near Villivalam.No. 93 on the same map. The إڑؤ“yؤپل¹ںu forms the southern boundary of the modern Conjeeveram tؤپlluqa and joins the Pؤپlؤپل¹ںu opposite Mؤ“lamaل¹‡appؤپkkam, which I have identified with Maل¹‡aل¹ںpؤپkkam, the western boundary of Koل¸چukoل¸·ل¸·i.

The executor (ؤپjأ±apti) of the grant was Ghإچraإ›arman (ll. 103 and 106), and the author of the Sanskrit portion, which, as in the Kإ«ram plates (l. 89) and the Udayؤ“ndiram plates (ll. 101 and 105), is called a praإ›asti or eulogy, was a certain Trivikrama (verse 31). To the Sanskrit portion is affixed a Tamil endorsement (l. 104 f.), which directs the inhabitants of إھل¹ںل¹ںukkؤپل¹­ل¹­u-kإچل¹­ل¹­am to execute the order of the king. The subsequent Tamil passage (l. 105 ff.) records that, on receipt of the royal order, the representatives of إھل¹ںل¹ںukkؤپل¹­ل¹­u-kإچل¹­ل¹­am marked the boundaries of the granted village under the guidance of their headman, and formally assigned all rights to the donee. Another Tamil sentence (l. 132 f.) states that the grant was executed in the presence of the local authorities (?), the ministers and the secretaries.

Then follow, in Sanskrit, three imprecatory verses (l. 133 ff.) and the statement that the document was written by His Majesty's great treasurer (l. 136). The inscription ends with a docket in Tamil (l. 137) and a few auspicious Sanskrit words.

Hail ! (Verse 1.) Victorious is the supreme Brahman, which is the cause of the production, stability and destruction of the three worlds; which is true, without end (and) without beginning; which consists of knowledge (alone); which is one; (and) which is the abode of immortality!

(2.) May that blessed Trivikrama (Vishل¹‡u) grant you prosperity, who, at the sacrifice of Bali, deceitfully asked (only) for three steps (of land), but suddenly expanded (and) strode thrice, (thereby) appropriating the world!

(3.) May Hara (إڑiva), the destroyer of Pura, increase your happiness, who bears the moon on his crest, who wears a serpent on his shoulder, who holds Bhavؤپnؤ« on his left, who bears affection to his worshipper, who bears Gaل¹…gؤپ on his head, who wears ashesDhإ«li seems to be used in the sense of bhإ«ti. on his body, who bears poison on his neck, who wears a braid in his hair, (and) who holds a spear in his hand!

(4.) May Trivikrama and Hara protect you, whose distinct (but) united bodies (respectively) bear on the neck the supreme splendour of two ornaments,â€”the kaustubha (jewel) and the black (poison),The word kؤپlikؤپ, â€کblackness,â€™ refers to the kؤپlakإ«ل¹­a poison. hold a discus and a spearAccording to Dr. Gundert's Malayؤپل¸·am Dictionary, paل¹­ل¹­asa is another form of the Sanskrit paل¹­ل¹­iإ›a, the Tamil forms of which are paل¹­ل¹­ayam or paل¹­ل¹­aiyam. for the destruction of the Daityas, are of black and white colour, (and) thrill with joy at the expansion (of the eyes) of إڑrؤ« and Gaurؤ«, (which emit) coquettish glances (resembling) arrows !

(5.) May Padmؤپ (Lakshmؤ«) regard you with fondness, who is seated on a lotus; whose pair of lotus hands is resplendent with a lotus; (and) whose excellent bath (is poured from) golden jars which are held by the trunks of (two) female elephants!

(6.) May that blessed ؤ€ryؤپ (Pؤپrvatؤ«), the sister of Vishل¹‡u, instantly remove dire adversity,â€”whom Cupid does not approach, out of fear, it seems, because he has observed the (third) eye on (her) forehead (and therefore takes her) for ؤھإ›vara!

(7.) May Vinؤپyaka (Gaل¹‡ؤ“إ›a) grant you freedom from obstacles, who is as white as the Kailؤپsa (mountain), whose girdle consists of a huge serpent, who has the face of an elephant, whose ears are large, who has a single big tusk, (and) whose eyes are (half closed as if he were) under the influence of rut!

(8.) May the race of the glorious Pallavas be protected for a long time by the supreme lords, those twofoldThe plural dvitayؤ“ is used in the same sense in the Raghuvaل¹پإ›a, viii. 89, as quoted in Bo7htlingk and Roth's Sanskrit-Wo7rterbuch, s.v. dvitaya:â€” drumasؤپnumatؤپل¹پ kimantaraل¹پ yadi vؤپyau dvitayepi te calؤپل¸¥. Mallinؤپtha explains dvitayepi by dviprakؤپrؤپ api. gods whom (they, viz., the Pallavas) have worshipped with traditional devotion,â€”(viz.) the gods in heaven who timely reward gifts, sacrifices and austerities, (and) the gods on earthI.e., the Brؤپhmaل¹‡as (bhإ«dؤ“va). who are engaged in the six duties, whose blessings are true, (and) who practise self-control!

(9.) The earth, surrounded by the rolling ocean, is conquered by the lord of men, who is the son of Hiraل¹‡ya (and) the lord of prosperity, whose crest is the bull, (and) the elephants of whose army ward off enemies.This verse refers, without mentioning the name itself, to king Nandivarman, whose father was Hiraل¹‡ya; see verses 28 and 30. The epithets which the king receives in the first half of the verse, are at the same time surnames of Brahmؤپ, Vishل¹‡u and إڑiva, and thus hint a comparison of the king to each of these three gods. As the Sؤپhityadarpaل¹‡a (pp. 103 and 107 of the Calcutta edition) expresses it, â€کthe ornament of simile is suggestedâ€™ (upamؤپlaل¹پkؤپro vyaل¹پgyaل¸¥); in another place (p. 109) the term upamؤپ-dhvani, â€کsuggestion of a simile,â€™ is used for this figure.

Hail! Adoration to إڑrؤ«! (10.) First, from the lotus which rose from the navel of Vishل¹‡u, was born the creator, whose origin is the (supreme) Brahman; who is self-existent; who fully knows the meaning of the sacred texts; (and) who has performed the creation of the whole world.

(11.) From him was born at the sacrifice a son of the mind alone,Compare Vol. I, No. 24, verse 2, where Aل¹…giras is called the son of the mind of Brahmؤپ. Aل¹…giras, who fully carried out his promises; who was more brilliant than fire; who, being sinless, put an end to sin; who, being the chief of seers, obtained a place among the Seven Seers; who reached (the highest degree of) austerities that can be desired; (and) who was the best axe for cutting the tree of ignorance.

(12.) From this Aل¹…giras (came Bل¹›ihaspati), who was an ocean of speeches (and) the father of politics; whom (Indra) the lord of the godsSukل¹›itin appears to be used for sumanas, â€کa god.â€™ (and) elder brother of Tridhؤپman (Vishل¹‡u), made his preceptor (guru); (and) relying on the power of whose intellect, the celestial women enjoy at ease amorous pleasures, without thinking of the rising and setting of the sun.

(13.) From him was born the fortunate (and) modest إڑaل¹پyu, who destroyed sin (and) resembled the sun in brilliancy. When Fire had disappeared, (he) became the fire of the gods and performed even the action of fire through his own power.

(14.) His son was a sage called Bharadvؤپja, who became the founder of the race (gإچtra) of the glorious Pallavas by the power of (his) virtues, (and) who mastered the three Vؤ“das, which resemble mountains, by (his) austerities.

(15.) From him came Drإچل¹‡a, the preceptorThis meaning of druhiل¹‡a is not found in the dictionaries. In Vol. I, No. 24, verse 3, the corresponding word is guru. of the Kurus, who was produced from the semenThe dictionaries do not contain this meaning of ambuja. (of Bharadvؤپja) in a pitcher called drإچل¹‡a The whose victorious banner was an altar painted on the skin of a black-buck; (and) who completely mastered (the four branches of) the science of archery,Bؤپل¹‡ؤپstra-vؤ“da is synonymous with dhanur-vؤ“da. which resemble the four oceans.

(16.) From him came the sage Aإ›vatthؤپman, who was an incarnation of (إڑiva) the enemy of Cupid; who deserved the confidence of the inhabitants of the world; (and) at the rising of whose anger, Kل¹›ishل¹‡a, Arjuna and Bhؤ«ma became terrified (and) threw down (their) weapons without any opposition.

(17.) The glorious Pallava, (during whose rule) the earth was untouched (even) by the smallest calamity,The same play on Pallava and ؤپpal-lava occurs in the Kإ«ram plates (line 11). The Udayؤ“ndiram plates (ll. 7 and 11) have vipal-lava instead of ؤپpal-lava. was suddenly born to him on a litter of sprouts (pallava)The same popular etymology of the name Pallava is alluded to in Vol. I, No. 32, verse 8. by (the nymph) Mؤ“nakؤپ,According to Vol. I, No. 32, verse 5, the mother of Pallava was the nymph Madanؤ«. that had been sent to him by إڑakra (Indra), who was afraid of (losing) his position (on account of the sage's austerities).

(18.) Though born from a race of Brؤپhmaل¹‡as, he possessed in the highest degree the valour of the Kshatل¹ںiyas, which was inherent in him. Does not the thunderbolt possess by nature the quality of burning, though it springs from the cloud ?

(19.) From him was produced Aإ›إچkavarman, who removed the distress of suppliant kings, (but) who distressed those who faced (him) in battle, (and) who, though bright as the moon, possessed a spotless fame (while the moon has a spot).

(Line 34.) From him descended the powerful, spotless race of the Pallavas, which resembled a partial incarnation of Vishل¹‡u, as it displayed unbroken courage in conquering the circle of the world with all its parts, (and) as it enforced the special rules of all castes and orders, and which resembled the descent of the Gaل¹…gؤپ (on earth), as it purified the whole world.

(Line 37.) All (the kings) sprung from this (race) possessed power that was everywhere irresistible, large armies, pure descent, birth from a lotus,See verse 10. (and) great piety, (and therefore) resembled Kumؤپra, whose spear is everywhere irresistible, (who is also called) Mahؤپsؤ“na, who is the son of Fire, who invented (the array of the army in the form of) a lotus, (and who is also called) Subrahmaل¹‡ya. The great fierceness,â€”that resembled fire,â€”of the power of their arms dried up,â€”like the water of the ocean,â€”the irresistible valour of all enemies. The spreading moonshine of their spotless fame removed the impurity of all the sins of the Kali age. Their extremely noble conduct and constant prosperity increased the affection of the crowd of their friends.This sentence has a second meaning, which refers to the sun (mitra), but which it is useless to reproduce in the translation. The beauty of their forms became the snare, in which,â€”like deer,â€”the hearts of young women (were caught). Their fame, like the fragrance of sandal trees, was pervading the southern region. The shadow of their (royal parasol) could not be crossed by the power of other (kings), just as the beauty of the celestial trees cannot be surpassed by the splendour of other (trees). They were full of splendour and kind to others, (and therefore) resembled the sun whose rays are beneficial to men. They experienced an increase (ؤپgama) of the affection (pratyaya) of (their) subjects (prakل¹›iti), and possessed blameless riches (vل¹›iddhi) and virtues (guل¹‡a), (and therefore) resembled the science of grammar, in which crude forms (prakل¹›iti), affixes (pratyaya) and augments (ؤپgama) are treated, (but) in which (the rules on) guل¹‡a and vل¹›iddhi are (not) without exceptions (apavؤپda).A similar إ›lؤ“sha occurs in the description of the Valabhؤ« king Dhruvasؤ“na II.; Ep. Ind., Vol. I, p. 91, note 28. See also إڑiإ›upؤپlavadha, xix. 75; Sؤپhityadarpaل¹‡a, paragraph 586; and Mr. S.P.Pandit's Preface to his edition of the Raghuvaل¹پإ›a, p. 45, note 1. They gave delight (nandana), but were without enemies (apa-ari-jؤپta); while the Nandana (garden) contains the pؤپrijؤپta (tree). Though full of learning (ghanؤپgama), they were not dull (jaل¸چa); while the rainy season (ghanؤپgama) brings water (jala). As Indra the heaven, (these) lords of the earth enjoyed the whole earth, which is bounded by the Chakravؤپla mountain (and) adorned by the seven continents and seven oceans.

(Line 45.) Among these have passed away in bygone times [S]kandavarman, Kal[i]ndavarman, Kؤپل¹‡agإچpa, Vishل¹‡ugإچpa, Vؤ«rakإ«[r]cha, Vؤ«rasiل¹پha, Siل¹پhavarman, Vishل¹‡usiل¹پha and other kings, who won great battles by (a knowledge of) the science of all weapons, whose valour was immeasurable, who had received by inheritance (the practice of) meritorious acts, who destroyed (the sins of) the Kali (age), whose minds were learned, whose bodies bore auspicious marks, who preserved (their) fame (free from blemishes), whose shoulders were fit to bear the whole earth, who were (evil) comets to (their) enemies, who conferred honours on (their) friends, (and) who were the barriers of all good conduct.

(Verse 20.) Thereafter came Siل¹پha[vishل¹‡u], the lion of the earth (Avanisiل¹پha), who was engaged in the destruction of enemies, (and) who vanquished the Malaya, Kaل¸·abhra, Mؤپlava, Chإچل¸·a and Pؤپل¹‡dya (kings), the Siل¹پhaل¸·a (king) who was proud of the strength of his arms, and the Kؤ“raل¸·as.

(21.) Then the earth was ruled by a king called Mahؤ“ndravarman, whose glory resembled that of Mahؤ“ndra, whose commands were respected (by all), (and) who annihilated (his) chief enemies at Puل¸·ل¸·alإ«ra.

(22.) From him was born the victorious hero Narasiل¹پhavarman, who surpassed the glory of the valour of Rؤپma by (his) conquest of Laل¹…kؤپ, who was a comet (that foreboded) destruction to the crowd of proud enemies, (and) who imitated the pitcher-born (Agastya) by (his) conquest of Vؤپtؤپpi.The same comparison occurs in the description of the reign of Narasiل¹پhavarman I. in the Kإ«ram plates (line 17) and in the Udayؤ“ndiram plates (l. 14).

(23.) From him was born Mahؤ“ndravarman, whose long arms were fierce thunderbolts to the crowd of enemies, (and) beginning with whom, meritorious acts for the benefit of temples and Brؤپhmaل¹‡as and (the use of) the vessel of the donorI.e., the vessel from which libations of water are poured out at donations. Compare the frontispiece of General Sir A. Cunningham's Coins of Ancient India, where such a vessel is figured in the hand of Anؤپthapiل¹‡ل¸چika. have highly prospered.

(24.) Then came Paramؤ“إ›varapإچtavarman, to whose desires the crowd of all kings was subject. This wonderful (king) possessed high prosperity (bhإ«ti), was the lord of men (bhإ«ta), had a bull for (his) crest (and) a club on (his) banner, (and) possessed immovable firmness, [thus resembling إڑiva, who wears sacred ashes (bhإ«ti), is the lord of goblins (Bhإ«ta), has a bull for his emblem and a club on his banner, and resides on the mountain].This is another case of upamؤپ-dhvani; see p. 354, note 5. The comparison with إڑiva is based on the name of the king, Paramؤ“إ›vara, which is at the same time one of the names of إڑiva.

(25.) From him was born a complete incarnation of the blessed Paramؤ“إ›vara, who equalled Narasiل¹پha both by (the strength of) his body and by (his) name (Narasiل¹پhavarman) that spread over the world. This crest-jewel of the Kshatriyas bestowed his wealth on temples and Brؤپhmaل¹‡as (and) devoutly caused the goddess of the earth, who was in his possession, to be enjoyed by those familiar with the four Vؤ“das.I.e., he made grants of land to learned Brؤپhmaل¹‡as.

(26.) From him came Paramؤ“إ›varapإچtavarman, who obtained desired treasures, (viz.) treasures of fame; who conquered the coquettish ways of the Kali (age); who led the way of policy, which had been prescribed by Dhishaل¹‡a (Bل¹›ihaspati); (and) who protected the worlds.

(27.) At present his prosperous kingdom, in which enemies are subdued by the power of (mere) commands, is ruled as far as the ocean by Nandivarman, who was chosen by the subjects, who is worthy of honour on account of (his) wisdom, (and) who is the full-moon of the race of the Pallavas, which is as extensive as the ocean.

(28.) His sixth (ancestor) was the lord Bhؤ«mavarman, who was the younger brother of, (and reigned) after, the glorious Siل¹پhavishل¹‡u; the fifth Pallava ruler (was) the glorious king Buddhavarman, praised by wise men; the fourth (was) ؤ€dityavarman, who resembled (Indra) the bearer of the thunderbolt; the third (was) Gإچvindavarman; (and) the second lord of the earth (was) the glorious Hiraل¹‡ya, the refuge of men.

(29.) This إڑrؤ«dharaThis was evidently a biruda of Nandivarman. resembles Vijaya (Arjuna) in battle, Karل¹‡ؤ«sutaAccording to a quotation of the commentator on the Kؤپdambarؤ« (Bombay edition of 1890, p. 40), Karل¹‡ؤ«suta or Karaل¹­aka was the author of a treatise on the art of thieving, and was, along with his two friends Vipula and Achala and his minister إڑaإ›a, mentioned in the Bل¹›ihatkathؤپ. He is also referred to in the Daإ›akumؤپracharita (Bombay edition of 1883, p. 48). in acquaintance with the arts, Rؤپma in archery, the king of VatsaCompare Vol. I, No. 25, paragraph 29. with respect to the science of elephants and to music, Kؤپma in (the opinion of) women, the first poet (Vؤپlmؤ«ki) in the composition of poetry, the master of policy (Bل¹›ihaspati) himself in suggesting expedients, (and) Dharma (Yudhishل¹­hira) in delighting the subjects.

(30.) Increasing in prosperity is our lord, king Nandivarman, who is able to support the race of the Pallavas; who is a born emperor; who is handsome; who is a master both in the art of gymnastics and in the virtue of modesty; who is the son of Hiraل¹‡ya; who belongs to the branch (varga) of Bhؤ«ma; who worships the feet of Hari (Vishل¹‡u); who is descended from a pure mother; who bears auspicious marks; who is the son of Rإچhiل¹‡ؤ«; (and) whose good deeds are numerous.

(Line 71.) While the twenty-second year of (his) reign was current, this Rؤپjؤپdhirؤپjaparamؤ“إ›vara, the Mahؤپrؤپja called Nandivarman, who is engaged in ruling the kingdom of Paramؤ“إ›varapإچtarؤپja; whose mind is clinging to, engaged in, and restless in (the desire for) supreme bliss; whose head is covered with dust that has dropped from the pair of lotus feet of the Lord (Paramؤ“shل¹­hin);This expression seems to refer to Vishل¹‡u, whose devotee Nandivarman was according to verse 30. who worships the gods, the Brؤپhmaل¹‡as, and (others) who are worthy of respect; who avoids the passions that oppress the people of the Kali age; who dries up (his) enemies by the fire of (his) growing valour; who refreshes (his) friends by the water of (his) growing affection; whose right hand is able (to fulfil) the vow of ruling the whole world; whose pair of feet is rubbed smooth (as it serves as) whetstones to the edges of the rubies in the diadems of all kings; who is gracefully embraced by the fortune of the Pallava race, (and who therefore resembles) the celestial tree, which is gracefully entwined by the creeper of the Nandana garden; the wrestler of warriors (Kshatriyamalla); the wrestler of the Pallavas (Pallavamalla); whose might is increasing in consequence of (his) meditations on the feet of the lord, (his) father;With bappa-bhaل¹­ل¹­ؤپraka-pؤپd-ؤپnudhyؤپna compare the similar expressions which Dr. Fleet quotes from three other Pallava inscriptions; Ind. Ant., Vol. XV, p. 274, 2nd column. In the Prؤپkل¹›it grant of إڑivaskandavarman occurs the instrumental plural mahؤپrؤپja-bappa-sؤپmؤ«hi; Ep. Ind., Vol. I, p. 6, text line 11. The nominative plural bappa-bhaل¹­ل¹­ؤپrakar occurs in line 88 of the Kإ«ram plates.
